notableRegulations: Alabama does not require a real estate license to wholesale as long as you have equitable interest in the property. Wholesalers must disclose their position as a principal in the transaction. The state has minimal regulations on assignment of contracts.
keyStatutes: Alabama Real Estate License Law (Title 34, Chapter 27), Alabama Uniform Residential Landlord Tenant Act
investorNotes: Alabama is one of the most investor-friendly states with low property taxes, quick non-judicial foreclosures, and strong landlord protections. Birmingham and Huntsville offer excellent cash-flow rental markets.

notableRegulations: Alaska allows wholesaling without a license provided the wholesaler has equitable interest. Contract assignments are permitted, but wholesalers should disclose their role to all parties. The market is small and deal volume is limited.
keyStatutes: Alaska Statutes Title 8, Chapter 88 (Real Estate Licensing), Alaska Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act
investorNotes: Alaska has a small real estate market but offers no state income tax and no transfer taxes. Anchorage is the primary market. High property values and remote locations make deal volume low but margins can be strong.

notableRegulations: Arizona permits wholesaling without a real estate license as long as the wholesaler holds equitable interest in the property via a purchase contract. Assignment of contracts is standard practice. Wholesalers must disclose their intent and role in the transaction.
keyStatutes: Arizona Revised Statutes Title 32, Chapter 20 (Real Estate), Arizona Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (ARS 33-1301)
investorNotes: Arizona is extremely investor-friendly with no transfer taxes, low LLC formation costs, and fast non-judicial foreclosures. Phoenix and Tucson are major wholesale markets. The state has seen rapid population growth driving demand.

notableRegulations: Arkansas allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal and commonly used. Wholesalers should disclose their position as a contract holder, not the property owner. Double closes are standard.
keyStatutes: Arkansas Code Title 17, Subtitle 4, Chapter 42 (Real Estate License Law), Arkansas Residential Landlord-Tenant Act
investorNotes: Arkansas offers low property taxes, affordable home prices, and fast foreclosures. Little Rock is the primary market. The state is landlord-friendly with no rent control. Very low LLC formation costs make it easy to get started.

notableRegulations: California allows wholesaling without a license, but wholesalers must exercise caution. Assigning contracts is legal provided the wholesaler has equitable interest. The state requires extensive disclosures in all real estate transactions. Wholesalers must avoid acting as unlicensed agents.
keyStatutes: California Business and Professions Code Division 4 (Real Estate), California Civil Code Section 1102 (Disclosures), California Revenue and Taxation Code
investorNotes: California is tenant-friendly with rent control in many cities. High property values create large assignment fees but also higher risk. The $800 annual LLC franchise tax is a significant cost. Los Angeles, San Diego, and Sacramento are active wholesale markets.

notableRegulations: Colorado allows wholesaling without a real estate license as long as the wholesaler has a legitimate purchase contract. Assignment of contracts is permitted. The state requires disclosure of the wholesaler's intent. Title companies in Colorado are familiar with wholesale transactions.
keyStatutes: Colorado Real Estate License Law (CRS 12-10-201), Colorado Revised Statutes Title 38 (Property)
investorNotes: Colorado has strong population growth and appreciation in Denver, Colorado Springs, and Fort Collins. Low LLC costs and low property taxes are attractive. The state leans tenant-friendly in metro areas. High median home prices require larger capital for double closes.

notableRegulations: Connecticut allows wholesaling without a license, but the state uses judicial foreclosures which slow down distressed inventory. Contract assignments are legal. Wholesalers should ensure proper disclosures to all parties.
keyStatutes: Connecticut General Statutes Chapter 392 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), CGS Chapter 832 (Landlord and Tenant)
investorNotes: Connecticut has high property taxes and slow judicial foreclosures. The state is tenant-friendly with strong protections. Hartford and Bridgeport offer lower entry points. No homestead exemption means creditors can pursue property equity.

notableRegulations: Delaware allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state is known for business-friendly laws and LLC protections. Contract assignments are standard. Judicial foreclosures slow down distressed property acquisition.
keyStatutes: Delaware Code Title 24, Chapter 29 (Real Estate Commission), Delaware Residential Landlord-Tenant Code (Title 25, Chapter 55)
investorNotes: Delaware is famous for business-friendly LLC laws. Low property taxes and no sales tax. Wilmington and Dover are the main markets. Judicial foreclosures add time but create negotiation opportunities. The annual LLC fee of $300 is higher than average.

notableRegulations: The District of Columbia allows wholesaling without a real estate license, but the market is highly regulated. Strong tenant protections and rent control laws affect investor strategies. Assignment of contracts is legal with proper disclosure.
keyStatutes: DC Code Title 42 (Real Property), DC Real Estate Licensure Act, DC Rental Housing Act
investorNotes: DC has high property values and strong appreciation. Tenant protections are among the strongest in the nation with rent control. The market is competitive but assignment fees can be substantial due to high property values. Transfer taxes are significant.

notableRegulations: Florida is one of the most active wholesale markets in the US. No license is required to wholesale with equitable interest. Assignment of contracts is standard practice. Florida requires disclosure of the wholesaler's role. Recent legislation has increased scrutiny on wholesaling practices.
keyStatutes: Florida Statutes Chapter 475 (Real Estate Brokers), Florida Statutes Chapter 83 (Landlord and Tenant), Florida Statutes Chapter 45 (Foreclosure)
investorNotes: Florida has no state income tax and one of the strongest homestead exemptions in the nation. Miami, Tampa, Jacksonville, and Orlando are major wholesale markets. Despite judicial foreclosures, deal volume is extremely high. Strong population growth supports demand.

notableRegulations: Georgia allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state has fast non-judicial foreclosures making distressed property acquisition efficient. Contract assignments are standard. Wholesalers must disclose their position in the transaction.
keyStatutes: Georgia Code Title 43, Chapter 40 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), Georgia Landlord Tenant Act (Title 44, Chapter 7)
investorNotes: Georgia is very investor-friendly with one of the fastest foreclosure timelines in the country. Atlanta is one of the top wholesale markets nationally. Landlord-friendly laws, no rent control, and strong population growth make it ideal for investors.

notableRegulations: Hawaii allows wholesaling without a license, but the market is unique due to leasehold vs. fee simple ownership structures. Contract assignments are legal. Wholesalers must understand the difference between leasehold and fee simple properties.
keyStatutes: Hawaii Revised Statutes Chapter 467 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), Hawaii Residential Landlord-Tenant Code (HRS Chapter 521)
investorNotes: Hawaii has the lowest property tax rate in the nation but extremely high property values. The leasehold system is unique. Judicial foreclosures are very slow. Tenant-friendly laws and high costs make this a challenging market for wholesalers. Honolulu is the primary market.

notableRegulations: Idaho allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state has seen rapid growth, especially in Boise. Contract assignments are legal and commonly used. Wholesalers should disclose their role in all transactions.
keyStatutes: Idaho Code Title 54, Chapter 20 (Real Estate Brokers), Idaho Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (Title 6, Chapter 3)
investorNotes: Idaho has seen explosive growth in Boise and surrounding areas. No transfer taxes and landlord-friendly laws attract investors. The market has become more competitive as prices have risen significantly. No annual LLC fee makes it cost-effective.

notableRegulations: Illinois requires a real estate license to wholesale in most cases due to strict interpretation of the Illinois Real Estate License Act. The state considers assigning contracts without a license as unlicensed brokerage activity. Wholesalers should obtain a license or use double-close strategies with legal counsel.
keyStatutes: Illinois Real Estate License Act of 2000 (225 ILCS 454), Illinois Residential Tenants' Right to Repair Act, Illinois Mortgage Foreclosure Law (735 ILCS 5/15-1101)
investorNotes: Illinois is one of the most challenging states for wholesalers due to license requirements and high property taxes. Chicago remains a major market despite regulations. Judicial foreclosures are slow. Cook County has some of the highest property taxes in the nation. Consult legal counsel before wholesaling.

notableRegulations: Indiana allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal and widely practiced. The state requires proper disclosure of the wholesaler's position. Indianapolis is one of the top wholesale markets in the Midwest.
keyStatutes: Indiana Code Title 25, Article 34.1 (Real Estate Brokers), Indiana Landlord-Tenant Law (IC 32-31)
investorNotes: Indiana is very investor-friendly with no transfer taxes and low property taxes. Indianapolis has a robust wholesale market with affordable entry points. Landlord-friendly laws and steady cash flow markets make it attractive. Despite judicial foreclosures, the state is efficient.

notableRegulations: Iowa allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are permitted. The state allows both judicial and non-judicial foreclosures, though non-judicial is more common for commercial properties. Proper disclosure is required.
keyStatutes: Iowa Code Chapter 543B (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), Iowa Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (Chapter 562A)
investorNotes: Iowa offers affordable real estate and steady rental markets. Des Moines and Cedar Rapids are the primary metros. Higher property taxes are offset by low entry points. The state is landlord-friendly with no rent control.

notableRegulations: Kansas allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state permits contract assignments with proper disclosure. Kansas uses judicial foreclosures which can extend timelines. The market offers affordable properties with good cash flow potential.
keyStatutes: Kansas Statutes Chapter 58, Article 30 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), Kansas Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (KSA 58-2540)
investorNotes: Kansas offers affordable properties and is landlord-friendly. Kansas City (KS side) and Wichita are the main markets. No transfer taxes reduce closing costs. The unlimited homestead exemption is one of the strongest in the country.

notableRegulations: Kentucky allows wholesaling without a real estate license provided the wholesaler has equitable interest. Contract assignments are legal. The state requires disclosures in residential transactions. Judicial foreclosures are standard.
keyStatutes: Kentucky Revised Statutes Chapter 324 (Real Estate Brokers), Kentucky Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (KRS 383.500-383.715)
investorNotes: Kentucky has very low LLC formation costs and affordable properties. Louisville and Lexington are the primary markets. Landlord-friendly laws and low taxes make it attractive for buy-and-hold investors. Judicial foreclosures add time but create opportunities.

notableRegulations: Louisiana allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state uses Napoleonic Code-based property law which differs from other states. Contract assignments are legal. The state uses judicial foreclosures with a right of redemption period.
keyStatutes: Louisiana Real Estate License Law (R.S. 37:1430-1470), Louisiana Civil Code (Book III, Titles I-III), Louisiana Landlord-Tenant Law (Civil Code Art. 2668-2729)
investorNotes: Louisiana uses unique Napoleonic Code-based property law. New Orleans and Baton Rouge are active markets. Low property taxes and no transfer taxes are attractive. The state's legal system requires understanding of community property and forced heirship rules.

notableRegulations: Maine allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal with proper disclosure. The state uses judicial foreclosures with a right of redemption. Maine has moderate tenant protections.
keyStatutes: Maine Real Estate Brokerage License Act (Title 32, Chapter 114), Maine Landlord-Tenant Law (Title 14, Chapter 710)
investorNotes: Maine has a smaller real estate market with Portland as the primary metro. Judicial foreclosures are slow, creating longer timelines. Higher property taxes and transfer taxes increase costs. Seasonal properties can offer unique wholesale opportunities.

notableRegulations: Maryland allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state has strong tenant protections. Contract assignments are legal. Maryland uses judicial foreclosures. The state has active wholesale markets in Baltimore and suburban DC areas.
keyStatutes: Maryland Real Estate Brokers Act (Business Occupations and Professions Article, Title 17), Maryland Real Property Code
investorNotes: Maryland has active wholesale markets, especially Baltimore which offers affordable properties near the DC metro. Tenant protections are strong. Transfer taxes and recordation taxes increase closing costs. The $300 annual LLC fee is notable.

notableRegulations: Massachusetts allows wholesaling without a real estate license, but the state has strong consumer protection laws. Contract assignments are legal. The state requires extensive disclosures. The Attorney General's office actively enforces consumer protection in real estate.
keyStatutes: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 112, Section 87PP-87DDD (Real Estate Brokers), MGL Chapter 186 (Landlord and Tenant), MGL Chapter 93A (Consumer Protection)
investorNotes: Massachusetts has high property values and strong tenant protections. Boston and surrounding areas are the primary markets. High LLC costs ($500 formation, $500 annual) are significant. The state has rent control in some cities. Slow judicial foreclosures add complexity.

notableRegulations: Michigan allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal and widely practiced, especially in Detroit. The state uses non-judicial foreclosures making distressed acquisition faster. Proper disclosure is required.
keyStatutes: Michigan Occupational Code (MCL 339.2501-2515, Real Estate Brokers), Michigan Truth in Renting Act, Michigan Housing Law of 1917
investorNotes: Michigan offers excellent wholesale opportunities, especially in Detroit and Grand Rapids. Fast non-judicial foreclosures create high deal volume. Low LLC costs make it easy to start. Higher property taxes are offset by very affordable home prices in many markets.

notableRegulations: Minnesota allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state permits contract assignments with disclosure. Non-judicial foreclosures are used with a redemption period. The state has moderate tenant protections.
keyStatutes: Minnesota Statutes Chapter 82 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), Minnesota Statutes Chapter 504B (Landlord and Tenant), Minnesota Statutes Chapter 580 (Foreclosure by Advertisement)
investorNotes: Minnesota has a stable real estate market centered on Minneapolis-St. Paul. No annual LLC fee is attractive. The state leans tenant-friendly in metro areas. Transfer taxes (deed tax) increase closing costs. Strong rental market due to the Twin Cities metro economy.

notableRegulations: Mississippi allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state has minimal regulations on contract assignments. Non-judicial foreclosures make distressed acquisition efficient. Disclosure requirements are standard.
keyStatutes: Mississippi Code Title 73, Chapter 35 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), Mississippi Residential Landlord and Tenant Act
investorNotes: Mississippi is very affordable with low property taxes and no transfer taxes. Jackson is the primary market. Fast non-judicial foreclosures and landlord-friendly laws make it attractive. Very low LLC costs. The market is smaller but offers strong cash flow potential.

notableRegulations: Missouri allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal and commonly practiced. The state uses non-judicial foreclosures. Kansas City and St. Louis are active wholesale markets with affordable properties.
keyStatutes: Missouri Revised Statutes Chapter 339 (Real Estate Brokers and Salesmen), Missouri Revised Statutes Chapter 441 (Landlord and Tenant)
investorNotes: Missouri is very investor-friendly with fast non-judicial foreclosures, no transfer taxes, and no annual LLC fees. Kansas City and St. Louis offer strong wholesale and rental markets. Landlord-friendly laws make property management straightforward.

notableRegulations: Montana allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal. The state uses non-judicial foreclosures. The market is smaller and primarily concentrated around Billings, Missoula, and Great Falls.
keyStatutes: Montana Code Annotated Title 37, Chapter 51 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), Montana Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (MCA 70-24-101)
investorNotes: Montana has no state sales tax and no transfer taxes. The market is smaller but growing, especially in Bozeman and Missoula. Landlord-friendly laws and no transfer taxes reduce investor costs. The state has seen significant appreciation in recent years.

notableRegulations: Nebraska allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal with proper disclosure. The state uses judicial foreclosures. Omaha and Lincoln are the primary markets.
keyStatutes: Nebraska Revised Statutes Chapter 76, Article 22 (Real Estate License Act), Nebraska Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (Sections 76-1401 to 76-1449)
investorNotes: Nebraska offers a stable, affordable market with Omaha as the primary metro. Higher property taxes are offset by low LLC costs and affordable home prices. Landlord-friendly laws support buy-and-hold strategies. Warren Buffett's home base speaks to the market's fundamentals.

notableRegulations: Nevada allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state has no income tax. Contract assignments are legal. Non-judicial foreclosures are fast. Las Vegas is one of the most active wholesale markets in the country.
keyStatutes: Nevada Revised Statutes Chapter 645 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), NRS Chapter 118A (Landlord and Tenant), NRS Chapter 107 (Non-judicial Foreclosure)
investorNotes: Nevada is very investor-friendly with no state income tax and low property taxes. Las Vegas and Reno are major markets. Fast non-judicial foreclosures create deal flow. The state has seen significant population growth driving demand. Some recent landlord regulations have been introduced in Clark County.

notableRegulations: New Hampshire allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state has no income tax or sales tax. Contract assignments are legal. Non-judicial foreclosures are used. The market is smaller and primarily concentrated around Manchester and Nashua.
keyStatutes: New Hampshire RSA Chapter 331-A (Real Estate Brokers and Salesmen), New Hampshire RSA Chapter 540 (Actions Against Tenants)
investorNotes: New Hampshire has no state income tax or sales tax, but property taxes are among the highest in the nation. Manchester and Nashua are the primary markets. Fast foreclosures and landlord-friendly laws help offset high tax costs.

notableRegulations: New Jersey allows wholesaling without a real estate license, but the state has strict regulations. The state uses judicial foreclosures with lengthy timelines. Strong tenant protections, including eviction restrictions, affect investors. Assignment of contracts is legal but must be properly disclosed.
keyStatutes: New Jersey Real Estate License Act (N.J.S.A. 45:15-1), New Jersey Anti-Eviction Act (N.J.S.A. 2A:18-61.1), New Jersey Fair Foreclosure Act
investorNotes: New Jersey has the highest property taxes in the nation and one of the slowest foreclosure processes. Tenant protections are very strong. Newark, Jersey City, and Camden offer lower entry points. High property values near NYC create large assignment fees. No homestead exemption is a notable risk factor.

notableRegulations: New Mexico allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal. The state uses judicial foreclosures with a redemption period. The market is smaller, centered around Albuquerque and Santa Fe.
keyStatutes: New Mexico Real Estate License Law (NMSA 61-29-1), New Mexico Uniform Owner-Resident Relations Act (NMSA 47-8-1)
investorNotes: New Mexico offers affordable properties with low LLC costs and no transfer taxes. Albuquerque is the primary market. Landlord-friendly laws and no annual LLC fee make it cost-effective. The market is smaller but steady with opportunities in value-add properties.

notableRegulations: New York allows wholesaling without a real estate license, but the state has strict regulations and some of the strongest tenant protections in the country. NYC has rent stabilization and rent control laws. Contract assignments are legal but must be disclosed. The Attorney General monitors real estate practices closely.
keyStatutes: New York Real Property Law, New York Real Property Actions and Proceedings Law, NYC Rent Stabilization Code, New York General Obligations Law (Section 5-703)
investorNotes: New York has one of the slowest foreclosure timelines in the nation. NYC is extremely tenant-friendly with rent control and stabilization. Upstate New York (Buffalo, Rochester, Syracuse) offers more affordable wholesale opportunities. High transfer taxes and property taxes affect margins. The market size is enormous despite regulatory challenges.

notableRegulations: North Carolina allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal and commonly used. The state uses non-judicial foreclosures. Wholesalers must disclose their position. The NC Real Estate Commission has provided guidance supporting wholesaling when done properly.
keyStatutes: North Carolina Real Estate License Law (NCGS Chapter 93A), North Carolina Residential Rental Agreements Act (NCGS Chapter 42)
investorNotes: North Carolina is one of the fastest-growing states for real estate investing. Charlotte and Raleigh-Durham are major markets with strong population growth. Landlord-friendly laws, non-judicial foreclosures, and moderate taxes make it very investor-friendly. The Research Triangle drives strong rental demand.

notableRegulations: North Dakota allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal. The state uses judicial foreclosures. The market is small, concentrated around Fargo, Bismarck, and Grand Forks.
keyStatutes: North Dakota Century Code Chapter 43-23 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), NDCC Chapter 47-16 (Landlord and Tenant)
investorNotes: North Dakota has a small but stable market. No transfer taxes. Fargo is the primary metro with a university-driven rental market. Oil industry activity in western ND can create opportunities. Landlord-friendly laws support rental investors.

notableRegulations: Ohio allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal and widely practiced. The state uses judicial foreclosures. Ohio has multiple active wholesale markets including Cleveland, Columbus, and Cincinnati.
keyStatutes: Ohio Revised Code Chapter 4735 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), Ohio Revised Code Chapter 5321 (Landlords and Tenants)
investorNotes: Ohio is a major wholesale market with very affordable properties, especially in Cleveland and Cincinnati. Landlord-friendly laws and no annual LLC fee are attractive. Higher property taxes are offset by extremely low entry points. Columbus is growing rapidly.

notableRegulations: Oklahoma has tightened regulations on wholesaling. The Oklahoma Real Estate Commission considers repeated assignment of contracts without a license to be unlicensed brokerage activity. A license is generally required for regular wholesale activity. Occasional contract assignments may be permitted, but consult legal counsel.
keyStatutes: Oklahoma Real Estate License Code (59 O.S. Section 858-100), Oklahoma Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (41 O.S. Section 101-136)
investorNotes: Oklahoma requires a real estate license for regular wholesale activity. Oklahoma City and Tulsa are the main markets. Low property taxes and no transfer taxes are attractive. Consult with an Oklahoma real estate attorney before wholesaling to ensure compliance.

notableRegulations: Oregon allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal. The state has implemented statewide rent control (the first in the US) limiting annual rent increases. Non-judicial foreclosures are used. Portland is the primary market.
keyStatutes: Oregon Revised Statutes Chapter 696 (Real Estate and Escrow Activities), Oregon Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (ORS Chapter 90), Oregon SB 608 (Rent Control)
investorNotes: Oregon has statewide rent control limiting annual rent increases. Portland and Eugene are the main markets. No sales tax but property taxes are moderate. Tenant-friendly laws affect rental investors. No transfer taxes reduce closing costs for wholesalers.

notableRegulations: Pennsylvania allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal. The state uses judicial foreclosures. Philadelphia and Pittsburgh are major wholesale markets with affordable properties. Transfer taxes are significant.
keyStatutes: Pennsylvania Real Estate Licensing and Registration Act (63 P.S. Section 455.101-455.902), Pennsylvania Landlord and Tenant Act (68 P.S. Section 250.101-250.602)
investorNotes: Pennsylvania has very affordable properties, especially in Philadelphia and Pittsburgh. Transfer taxes are among the highest in the country (2% state plus local). No annual LLC fee is attractive. Philadelphia has strong tenant protections. No homestead exemption.

notableRegulations: Rhode Island allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state allows both judicial and non-judicial foreclosures. Contract assignments are legal. Providence is the primary market. Tenant protections are moderate.
keyStatutes: Rhode Island General Laws Title 5, Chapter 20.5 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), RI General Laws Title 34, Chapter 18 (Residential Landlord and Tenant Act)
investorNotes: Rhode Island allows both judicial and non-judicial foreclosures. Providence is the main market with affordable properties. Higher property taxes are offset by proximity to Boston. The state is small but has active investor interest.

notableRegulations: South Carolina allows wholesaling but has increased scrutiny of the practice. The South Carolina Real Estate Commission has issued guidance that repeated marketing of properties under contract without a license may constitute unlicensed brokerage. Wholesalers should proceed with caution and consult legal counsel.
keyStatutes: South Carolina Real Estate License Law (SC Code Title 40, Chapter 57), South Carolina Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (SC Code Title 27, Chapter 40)
investorNotes: South Carolina has increasing scrutiny of wholesaling practices. Charleston, Columbia, and Greenville are growing markets. Low property taxes are very attractive. Landlord-friendly laws support rental investors. Consult legal counsel about current wholesaling regulations.

notableRegulations: South Dakota allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal. The state uses judicial foreclosures. The market is small, centered around Sioux Falls and Rapid City. No state income tax.
keyStatutes: South Dakota Codified Laws Title 36, Chapter 21A (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), SDCL Chapter 43-32 (Landlord and Tenant)
investorNotes: South Dakota has no state income tax and is landlord-friendly. Sioux Falls is the primary market with steady growth. The market is small but stable. Unlimited homestead exemption is very protective for investors who live in the state.

notableRegulations: Tennessee allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal and commonly used. Non-judicial foreclosures make distressed property acquisition fast. No state income tax. Nashville and Memphis are major wholesale markets.
keyStatutes: Tennessee Real Estate Broker License Act (TCA Title 62, Chapter 13), Tennessee Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (TCA Title 66, Chapter 28)
investorNotes: Tennessee is extremely investor-friendly with no state income tax, fast foreclosures, and landlord-friendly laws. Nashville and Memphis are top-tier wholesale markets. The LLC costs ($300 formation and annual) are higher than some states. Strong population growth in Nashville drives demand.

notableRegulations: Texas does not require a real estate license to wholesale as long as the wholesaler holds equitable interest via a purchase contract. Contract assignments are standard practice. Texas law requires disclosure of the wholesaler's position. The Texas Real Estate Commission has clarified that wholesaling with equitable interest is not brokerage.
keyStatutes: Texas Property Code, Texas Business and Commerce Code, Texas Occupations Code Chapter 1101 (Real Estate License Act)
investorNotes: Texas is one of the most investor-friendly states with no state income tax, no transfer taxes, and the fastest foreclosure timeline in the nation (27 days). Dallas, Houston, San Antonio, and Austin are major wholesale markets. High property taxes are the main cost consideration. Strong homestead protections.

notableRegulations: Utah allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal and widely practiced. The state uses non-judicial foreclosures. Salt Lake City and Provo are the primary markets with strong population growth.
keyStatutes: Utah Code Title 61, Chapter 2f (Real Estate Licensing and Practices Act), Utah Fit Premises Act (Utah Code 57-22)
investorNotes: Utah is investor-friendly with low property taxes, no transfer taxes, and low LLC costs. Salt Lake City has seen rapid appreciation. The state has strong population growth and a diversified economy. Landlord-friendly laws support rental investors.

notableRegulations: Vermont allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal. The state uses judicial foreclosures. The market is small and primarily rural. Burlington is the primary metro area.
keyStatutes: Vermont Statutes Title 26, Chapter 41 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespeople), Vermont Statutes Title 9, Chapter 137 (Residential Rental Agreements)
investorNotes: Vermont has a small, primarily rural real estate market. High property taxes and slow judicial foreclosures make it challenging. Burlington is the only significant metro. Transfer taxes add to closing costs. Tenant-friendly laws are in place.

notableRegulations: Virginia allows wholesaling without a real estate license as long as the wholesaler has equitable interest. Contract assignments are legal and commonly practiced. Non-judicial foreclosures make distressed acquisitions efficient. The Virginia Real Estate Board has not restricted wholesaling.
keyStatutes: Virginia Real Estate Licensing Law (Code of Virginia Title 54.1, Chapter 21), Virginia Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (VRLTA, Code of Virginia Title 55.1, Chapter 12)
investorNotes: Virginia is investor-friendly with fast non-judicial foreclosures and landlord-friendly laws. Northern Virginia (near DC), Richmond, and Virginia Beach are major markets. The state has a strong economy and steady population growth. Moderate LLC costs.

notableRegulations: Washington allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal. The state uses non-judicial foreclosures. No state income tax. Seattle and Tacoma are major markets. The state has implemented a capital gains tax on real estate profits over $250,000.
keyStatutes: Washington Real Estate Brokerage Relationships Act (RCW 18.86), Washington Residential Landlord-Tenant Act (RCW 59.18), Washington Deed of Trust Act (RCW 61.24)
investorNotes: Washington has no state income tax but has implemented a capital gains tax on profits over $250K. Seattle is a high-value market. Tacoma and Spokane offer more affordable entry points. Recent tenant-friendly legislation has increased protections. Transfer taxes (excise tax) are based on sale price.

notableRegulations: West Virginia allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal. The state uses non-judicial foreclosures. The market is affordable with very low entry points. Charleston and Huntington are the primary metros.
keyStatutes: West Virginia Code Chapter 30, Article 40 (Real Estate Brokers), West Virginia Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (WV Code Chapter 37, Article 6A)
investorNotes: West Virginia has some of the most affordable real estate in the country. Low property taxes and fast non-judicial foreclosures are attractive. Charleston is the primary market. The state has low population growth, which limits appreciation potential but creates cash flow opportunities.

notableRegulations: Wisconsin allows wholesaling without a real estate license. Contract assignments are legal. The state uses judicial foreclosures with a redemption period. Milwaukee and Madison are the primary markets. The state has moderate tenant protections.
keyStatutes: Wisconsin Statutes Chapter 452 (Real Estate Practice), Wisconsin Statutes Chapter 704 (Landlord and Tenant), Wisconsin Statutes Chapter 846 (Foreclosure of Mortgages)
investorNotes: Wisconsin has slow judicial foreclosures and moderate-to-high property taxes. Milwaukee offers very affordable properties with strong rental demand. Madison is a growing market with university-driven demand. Tenant protections are moderate to strong.

notableRegulations: Wyoming allows wholesaling without a real estate license. The state is known for strong business and privacy protections. No state income tax. Contract assignments are legal. The market is small, centered around Cheyenne and Casper.
keyStatutes: Wyoming Statutes Title 33, Chapter 28 (Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons), Wyoming Residential Rental Property Act (WS 1-21-1201)
investorNotes: Wyoming has no state income tax and strong business privacy protections. Cheyenne and Casper are the primary markets. The state is very business-friendly with low taxes. The market is small but landlord-friendly laws and no transfer taxes reduce investor costs.
